entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
The discussion highlights several critical aspects of bitcoin's value and utility that extend beyond mere transactional capabilities. Bitcoin is appreciated for its properties such as the ability to finalize transactions quickly, the absence of counterparty risks, lower fees compared to traditional third-party intermediaries, and the choice it offers users against other currencies or payment systems. These characteristics contribute significantly to its perceived value among users who opt for bitcoin rather than conventional financial mechanisms.
Further insights were shared regarding the intrinsic benefits that long-term holders of bitcoin experience. It was pointed out that these benefits are not solely tied to conducting transactions but also arise from holding the cryptocurrency over extended periods. This perspective challenges the narrower view that bitcoin’s worth is primarily from transactional utility and emphasizes the broader value gained by its holders.
Additionally, the conversation touched upon the governance of bitcoin, specifically how block templates are created by miners and the implications of including transactions in non-standard ways. There is an acknowledgment that while miners have the freedom to decide on the inclusion of transactions, doing so in unconventional ways could lead to financial losses, thus disincentivizing them from deviating from norms. The conversation also delved into the distribution of costs for bitcoin's security. Currently, these costs are borne mainly by those engaging in transactions. However, since various stakeholders derive value from bitcoin, there is a notion that the responsibility for security expenses should be more evenly distributed among all users, aligning with the proportional value they receive. This setup, though ideal, is complicated by the economic realities that discourage miners from making unprofitable decisions to achieve such an equitable distribution.
